speaker: Unknown
en: Before, it was very easy to always say, "Well, I will never be able to do this because insert skill issue."
zh: 以前，总是很容易说，“嗯，我永远做不到这一点，因为插入技能问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: We're realizing that even if you have the skills at your fingertips, the thing that matters is agency.
zh: 我们正在意识到，即使你拥有触手可及的技能，重要的是智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think agency is very evenly distributed in the world.
zh: 我不认为智能体在这个世界上分布得很均匀。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have a piece of advice for someone that wants to develop this within themselves?
zh: 对于想要在内心培养这种能力的人，你有什么建议吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I tell this to myself, "Hey, do you drive notion like it's stolen?"
zh: 我告诉自己，“嘿，你是不是像偷来的一样驱动 Notion？”

speaker: Unknown
en: One day you wake up and you realize the world is made up by people no smarter than you.
zh: 有一天你醒来，你意识到世界是由那些不比你聪明的人组成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It just really awakens you to the idea that you can't just change things.
zh: 这真的唤醒了你的一种想法，即你不能只是改变事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about your job a couple years ago, what's most changed?
zh: 如果回想你几年前的工作，改变最大的是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: The first 10% of every project are now free.
zh: 现在每个项目的前 10% 都是免费的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It takes almost no effort to now build the first version of a startup.
zh: 现在构建初创公司的第一个版本几乎不需要任何努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Taste comes up a lot now.
zh: 品味现在经常被提及。

speaker: Unknown
en: Taste actually means you're able to run a virtual machine in your head where given an idea, you can predict for a certain in group whether they're going to like it or not.
zh: 品味实际上意味着你能够在你的脑海中运行一台虚拟机，给定一个想法，你能够预测某个特定内部群体是否会喜欢它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just have to do reps.
zh: 你只需要做重复练习。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's almost like training a model.
zh: 这几乎就像训练一个模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you think matters to building a successful product?
zh: 你认为对于构建一个成功的产品来说什么重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: All the great products have something tiny that is a superpower, one tiny core that is so exceptionally good.
zh: 所有伟大的产品都有一个微小的东西是超能力，一个微小的核心是如此出色。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the biggest pitfalls is if you get into the loop of, if I just add one more thing to the product, it'll be finally great.
zh: 最大的陷阱之一是如果你陷入了这种循环，如果我在产品中再增加一样东西，它终于会很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: That never works.
zh: 那永远行不通。

speaker: Unknown
en: Give this hot take on universal basic income.
zh: 给出关于普遍基本收入的热门观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: We already have universal basic income.
zh: 我们已经有了普遍基本收入。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's called knowledge work.
zh: 这被称为知识工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Max Max Schoening.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Max Max Schoening。

speaker: Unknown
en: Max is a hard person to describe.
zh: Max 很难描述。

speaker: Unknown
en: He was a product manager at Google.
zh: 他曾是 Google 的产品经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: He ran the design team at Heroku.
zh: 他领导了 Heroku 的设计团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: He was a design leader and an engineer at GitHub under Nat Friedman.
zh: 他曾是 GitHub 的设计负责人和工程师，在 Nat Friedman 手下。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's also a two-time founder and is now head of product at Notion.
zh: 他也是一位两次创业的创始人，现在是 Notion 的产品负责人。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's one of the most successful AI forward product leaders out there.
zh: 他是目前最成功的 AI 先锋产品领导者之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as you'll soon see, one of the deepest thinkers on how AI impacts how we build and how we use software.
zh: 正如你很快将看到的，他是关于 AI 如何影响我们构建软件和使用软件的最深刻的思想家之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out lennysproductpass.com for an insane set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在我们开始之前，别忘了查看 lennysproductpass.com ，那里有一系列专门为 Lenny 的通讯订阅者提供的疯狂优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Max Schoening.
zh: 有了这个，我为你带来 Max Schoening。

speaker: Unknown
en: Max, welcome to the podcast.
zh: Max，欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thank you for having me.
zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am so excited to have you here.
zh: 我非常兴奋你能来到这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like there's this quote I think about when I think about you and you being on this podcast.
zh: 我觉得有一句话，当我想到你和你来到这个播客时，我会想到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It comes from the Bible.
zh: 它来自圣经。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just paraphrasing, the quote is, "I was made for such a time as this."
zh: 只是意译一下，这句话是，“我为这样的时刻而造。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like there's all this talk about roles merging, designers becoming PMs, engineers, everyone's the same, the Venn diagrams collapsing.
zh: 我觉得现在有太多关于角色融合的讨论，设计师变成产品经理（PM），工程师，每个人都是一样的，韦恩图在崩溃。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've been that for a long time.
zh: 你很久以前就是这样了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's hard to even describe what you are and what you've done.
zh: 甚至很难描述你是什么以及你做了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've done all the things.
zh: 你做过所有的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I feel like you have such a unique insight into where things are heading.
zh: 所以我觉得你对事物的发展方向有着独特的见解。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to start with just this broad question.
zh: 我想先从这个宽泛的问题开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: What have you seen about where things are going for product teams for product building as AI becomes more powerful, as we integrate it more into our workflows?
zh: 关于产品团队和产品构建的发展方向，随着 AI 变得更强大，随着我们将其更多地集成到工作流中，你看到了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I ask you this because I've heard from so many people at Notion that you are the reason that designers are shipping code, PMs are shipping code.
zh: 我问你这个问题是因为我从 Notion 的很多人那里听说，你是设计师在发布代码、产品经理在发布代码的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're not just living in the future, you're pushing the whole team and company to live in the future.
zh: 你不仅仅是生活在未来，你正在推动整个团队和公司去生活在未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so coming back to the question, just like, what are you seeing about where things are going?
zh: 所以回到这个问题，就像，你关于事情的发展方向看到了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What will change?
zh: 什么会改变？

speaker: Unknown
en: What will people realize in the next few months, years that you're already seeing?
zh: 在接下来的几个月、几年里，人们会意识到什么，而你已经看到了？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, first of all, when you said a quote from the Bible, I was very curious where this was going to head.
zh: 嗯，首先，当你说是来自圣经的一句话时，我很好奇这会导向何方。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the first time I've quoted the Bible on this podcast, I think.
zh: 我想这是我在这个播客上第一次引用圣经。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wouldn't take credit for the designers at Notion and the PMs at Notion now code.
zh: 我不会把 Notion 的设计师和 Notion 现在 code 的产品经理归功于我。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that would have probably happened anyways, but I can tell you the origin story of it, which is when I joined Notion, we were building a lot of chat interfaces and we were designing the chat interfaces in Figma.
zh: 我想那无论如何都可能发生，但我可以告诉你它的起源故事，那就是当我加入 Notion 时，我们正在构建很多聊天界面，并且我们在 Figma 中设计这些聊天界面。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's this great talk by Bret Victor, Stop Drawing Dead Fish, which essentially is ...
zh: Bret Victor 有一个很棒的演讲，叫《Stop Drawing Dead Fish》，本质上就是……

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, the static image of a chat is basically the dead fish here.
zh: 我是说，聊天的静态图像基本上就是这里的死鱼。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to feel the AI to some degree.
zh: 你必须在某种程度上感受 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so two designers, myself, just put together the worst possible playground you could think of of a small code base that is very LLM-friendly, used the tools that LLMs are very good at using.
zh: 于是两位设计师和我自己，只是拼凑出了你能想到的最糟糕的游乐场，它是一个对 LLM 非常友好的小型代码库，使用了 LLM 非常擅长使用的工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we moved all of our prototyping for specifically the chat interfaces to that.
zh: 然后我们将所有的原型设计，特别是针对聊天界面的，都转移到了那个上面。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just to understand this playground concept, essentially, this is an idea of people work within this separate area with AI tools versus their whole Notion code base, making it really easy to get started and try stuff.
zh: 为了理解这个游乐场的概念，本质上，这是一个人们在独立于他们整个 Notion 代码库的独立区域中使用 AI 工作的想法，这使得开始尝试东西变得非常容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was the first version.
zh: 那是第一个版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: It aligned with model capabilities at the time.
zh: 它与当时的模型能力相一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't always use ... maybe at Notion, the main code base is not always the most agent-friendly because iterations and a decade of patterns.
zh: 我们并不总是使用……也许在 Notion，主代码库并不总是最对智能体友好的，因为有一 decade 的模式和迭代。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we optimized for, okay, how can we make this the least scary and most one-shottable so that people would just have to overcome this oh, the fear of the terminal, but then it just becomes chatting.
zh: 所以我们进行了优化，好吧，我们要如何让它变得最不可怕并且最“一次性可做”，以至于人们只需要克服对终端的恐惧，然后它就变成了聊天。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we recreated a bunch of the patterns and UIs that exist in that playground.
zh: 我们在那个游乐场中重新创建了一堆现存的模式和 UI。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, the good news is that's just to get people on the treadmill because as model capabilities get better, now we have the same designers and PMs also just contributing to the production code base to a lesser degree, of course.
zh: 现在的好消息是，这只是为了让人们上跑步机，因为随着模型能力变强，现在我们也有同样的设计师和产品经理在某种程度上也在为生产代码库做贡献，当然程度较轻。

speaker: Unknown
en: But like you can see where the trend is headed as model capabilities get better.
zh: 但你可以看到随着模型能力的提高，趋势正在向哪个方向发展。

speaker: Unknown
en: The amount of work that you can do is obviously going to increase exponentially.
zh: 你能做的工作量显然将呈指数级增长。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by our season's presenting sponsor WorkOS.
zh: 本集由我们的本季展示赞助商 WorkOS 赞助播出。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, Replit, Sierra, Clay, and hundreds of other winning companies all have in common?
zh: OpenAI、Anthropic、Cursor、Vercel、Replit、Sierra、Clay 以及数百家其他成功公司有什么共同点？

speaker: Unknown
en: They are all powered by WorkOS.
zh: 它们都由 WorkOS 提供支持。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're building a product for the enterprise, you've felt a pain of integrating single sign-on, skim, RBAC, audit logs, and other features required by large companies.
zh: 如果你在为企业构建产品，你一定感受过集成单点登录（single sign-on）、skim、RBAC、审计日志以及大公司要求的其他功能的痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS turns those deal blockers into drop-in APIs with a modern developer platform built specifically for B2B SaaS.
zh: WorkOS 利用专门为 B2B SaaS 构建的现代化开发者平台，将这些交易阻碍变成了即插即用的 API。

speaker: Unknown
en: Literally, every startup that I'm an investor in that starts to expand upmarket ends up working with WorkOS, and that's because they are the best.
zh: 说实话，我所投资的每一家开始向高端市场扩张的初创公司最终都与 WorkOS 合作，因为他们是最优秀的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you are a seed stage startup trying to land your first enterprise customer or a unicorn expanding globally, WorkOS is the fastest path to becoming enterprise ready and unblocking growth.
zh: 无论你是试图拿下第一个企业客户的种子轮初创公司，还是正在全球扩张的独角兽，WorkOS 都是成为企业就绪并解锁增长的最快途径。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's essentially striped for enterprise features.
zh: 它本质上就是用于企业功能的 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit workos.com to get started or just hit up their Slack where they have actual engineers waiting to answer your questions.
zh: 访问 workos.com 开始使用，或者直接联系他们的 Slack，那里有真正的工程师等待回答你的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Work OS allows you to build faster with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许你用令人愉悦的 API、全面的文档和流畅的开发者体验更快地构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise ready today.
zh: 前往 workos.com 让你的应用今天就为企业做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe give us a sense of where things are today, like how much are designers shipping stuff, PMs, and then just what do you see about where things might be heading seeing all this actually happening at a company like Notion?
zh: 也许你可以给我们一个关于现状的感觉，比如设计师和产品经理正在发布多少东西，然后鉴于你在 Notion 这样的公司看到了所有这些实际发生，你认为事情可能会向哪里发展？

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel so uncomfortable predicting the future in terms of where things are heading because, well, predicting exponentials is really hard, but I'll take the stab at, it is very, very useful for designers to move from manipulating Figma documents into code.
zh: 我对预测事情的发展方向感到很不舒服，因为预测指数级增长真的很难，但我愿意尝试一下，对设计师来说从操作 Figma 文档转向代码是非常、非常有用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That has always been useful.
zh: 这一直都有用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've always been camp designers should code.
zh: 我一直属于设计师应该写代码的阵营。

speaker: Unknown
en: In a previous life, I led design and product at GitHub and GitHub designers before LLMs contributed to GitHub, I think and the top contributors to GitHub itself, like 10% were designers.
zh: 在上一段生活中，我在 GitHub 领导设计和产品，在 LLM 出现之前，GitHub 的设计师就为 GitHub 做出了贡献，我认为 GitHub 本身的主要贡献者中，大约 10% 是设计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right now, processes are breaking.
zh: 现在，流程正在破裂。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is we have designers who now mostly code and prototype in code, and then they are asked by other teams in marketing and so on to reverse engineer that in Figma because they use that to create assets for videos and so on.
zh: 一是我们有设计师现在主要在代码中编写代码和原型，然后他们被营销团队等其他团队要求在 Figma 中进行逆向工程，因为他们用那个来为视频等创建素材。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so obviously, that is kind of silly.
zh: 所以显然，这有点傻。

speaker: Unknown
en: That seems like busy work.
zh: 这看起来像是瞎忙活。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the pushing to production, I think it's a spectrum, obviously small changes, styling tweaks and so on.
zh: 关于推送到生产环境，我认为这是一个谱系，显然对于微小的改动、样式调整等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a given that you can just do that now.
zh: 现在做这些是理所当然的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do have a general maybe issue with vibe coding in the sense of I don't feel like the quality of software has increased all that much in the last 12 months.
zh: 我确实对 vibe coding 有一个普遍的问题，在某种意义上，我不觉得软件的质量在过去的 12 个月里有太大的提升。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think maybe the amount of software has, but it's very, very hard to find software that is reliable.
zh: 我认为也许软件的数量增加了，但很难找到可靠的软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the way we see it is it's not so much about pushing to production and having designers deploy.
zh: 所以我们看待它的方式并不太是关于推送到生产环境并让设计师部署。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's about them thinking and designing in the medium that will actually end up being the real thing once engineering takes it over.
zh: 而是关于他们在一种媒介中思考和设计，这种媒介最终一旦工程部门接手，就会成为真正的实物。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's all this talk about designers should be shipping code, PM should be shipping code.
zh: 有很多关于设计师应该发布代码、产品经理应该发布代码的讨论。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's the flip side of, because engineers can move so fast, there's so much more happening, things are moving all the time.
zh: 然后还有另一面，因为工程师移动得非常快，发生的事情更多，东西一直在移动。

speaker: Unknown
en: Designers and PMs are squeezed more and more because it's hard to stand top of all these things that are constantly shipping.
zh: 设计师和产品经理被挤压得越来越多，因为很难跟上所有这些不断发布的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so maybe it doesn't actually make sense for designers and PMs to be spending time coding.
zh: 所以也许设计师和产品经理花时间编码实际上并没有意义。

speaker: Unknown
en: And instead, their time is better spent making sure things are moving in a direction that makes sense for the business.
zh: 相反，他们的时间最好花在确保事情朝着对业务有意义的方向发展。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's cohesive.
zh: 它是有凝聚力的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your thoughts on just that balance?
zh: 你对这种平衡有什么看法？

speaker: Unknown
en: I actually don't care at all whether designers write code that lands in production.
zh: 我其实一点也不在乎设计师编写的代码是否进入生产环境。

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason I like thinking in code is because it forces you to consider the medium.
zh: 我喜欢用代码思考的原因是因为它迫使你考虑媒介。

speaker: Unknown
en: If then all of that gets thrown out, great.
zh: 如果所有那些东西都被扔掉了，那很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, for example, I think the two extremes would be if a PM or a designer knows how to tweak with, pick your favorite, they're all the same, Codex, Claude Code or whatever.
zh: 所以，例如，我认为这两个极端是，如果 PM 或设计师知道如何微调，选你喜欢的，它们都一样，Codex、Claude Code 或者其他的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If they know how to tweak small details of the UI, but they don't understand how an agent loop works, I would much rather take the designer or PM that deeply has an affinity for understanding how agent loops work and can design those than someone who can write traditional software and tweak styles.
zh: 如果他们知道如何微调 UI 的小细节，但他们不理解 agent loop 是如何工作的，我宁愿要那个深深对理解 agent loop 工作原理有亲和力并能设计它们的设计师或 PM，而不是那个能编写传统软件和微调样式的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's really hard because I think the only way that you can actually get to understanding agent loops is if you build them in the material that they're made of, which is currently code.
zh: 这真的很难，因为我认为你真正理解 agent loop 的唯一途径是，用构成它们的材料来构建它们，目前那就是代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: And increasingly so if you look at all the coding harnesses, basically, the operating systems of the '90s.
zh: 而且越来越如此，如果你看所有的 coding harnesses，基本上就是 90 年代的操作系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, I think that's why I care that people code, not because of the utility of shipping to production, but because it forces you to really interrogate the material that you're designing with.
zh: 所以，我认为这就是为什么我在乎人们写代码，不是因为交付到生产环境的实用性，而是因为它迫使你真正审问你正在设计的材料。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's more the prototyping use cases than we're just going to be shipping more features because we can.
zh: 所以这更多的是原型制作的用例，而不是我们要因为能做就发布更多功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: It tends to be that once you awaken someone to a new material, that at some point they also blur the lines and then write production code.
zh: 往往情况是，一旦你唤醒某人去接触一种新材料，在某个点他们也会模糊界限，然后编写生产代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think it's really important not to forget that the reason why is to become a master of the material, not a cog in the delivery mechanism for the idea.
zh: 但我认为非常重要的是不要忘记，原因是成为材料的大师，而不是想法交付机制中的一个齿轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is really interesting.
zh: 那真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you find is key to people being successful in this new world?
zh: 你发现人们在这个新世界中取得成功的关键是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of designers, a lot of PMs at Notion.
zh: Notion 有很多设计师，很多 PM。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you find is separating the ones that are thriving and will do well in this coming future versus ones that may fall behind?
zh: 你发现是什么区分了那些在这个即将到来的未来中茁壮成长并会做得好的人，和那些可能掉队的人？

speaker: Unknown
en: I suspect that this is also something that has always been the case, and we would just categorize this as founder versus not.
zh: 我怀疑这也是一直如此的事情，我们只会将其归类为创始人与非创始人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And do you start a startup versus not, which is agency.
zh: 以及你是否创业，这就是 agency。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think before, it was very easy to always say, "Well, I will never be able to do this because insert skill issue."
zh: 我认为以前，总是很容易说，“好吧，我永远做不到这一点，因为插入技能问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we're realizing that even if you have the skills at your fingertips because now, I don't know, an AGI adjacent model helps you.
zh: 我认为我们正在意识到，即使你拥有触手可及的技能，因为现在，我不知道，一个 AGI adjacent 模型会帮助你。

speaker: Unknown
en: The thing that matters is agency, and I don't think agency is very evenly distributed in the world.
zh: 重要的是 agency，我不认为 agency 在世界上分布得很均匀。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think people who have true agency and they understand that the world around them is malleable will do great.
zh: 我认为那些拥有真正 agency 并且理解他们周围的世界是可塑的人会做得很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the folks who stick to, "Tell me really, what does it mean to be a PM?
zh: 而那些坚持的人，“告诉我，做 PM 意味着什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What does it mean to be a designer?
zh: 做设计师意味着什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what's my job as an engineer?"
zh: 我作为工程师的工作是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that will be much harder and cultivate agency.
zh: 我认为这将会难得多，并且培养 agency。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's the thing.
zh: 我认为那就是关键。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there an example of someone using agency ... a good example at Notion of someone just leaning into that and doing ... and maybe shipping something, changing the way something was happening at Notion, just to give us a like, "Oh, wow.
zh: 有没有人使用 agency 的例子……一个 Notion 的好例子，有人只是拥抱那个并做了……也许发布了一些东西，改变了 Notion 某些事情的发生方式，只是为了给我们一个像，“噢，哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: I see what you're talking about"?
zh: 我明白你在说什么”？

speaker: Unknown
en: Notinos are, this was surprising to me, especially on the design team way above average agency compared to other places that I've worked at.
zh: Notinos 是，这对我来说很令人惊讶，特别是设计团队，与其他我工作过的地方相比，拥有远高于平均水平的 agency。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Notinos, by the way, are Notion employees.
zh: 顺便说一句，Notinos 就是 Notion 的员工。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, sorry.
zh: 是的，抱歉。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, cool.
zh: 好的，酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: [inaudible 00:12:17].

speaker: Unknown
en: I would say one example would be someone like Brian Levin, who you should probably have on the podcast at some point.
zh: 我说一个例子就是像 Brian Levin 这样的人，你应该在某个时候让他上这个播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: He was on our sister podcast, How I AI.
zh: 他上过我们的姊妹播客，How I AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll link to that episode.
zh: 我们会链接到那一集。

speaker: Unknown
en: There we go.
zh: 就这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You should cut this one short and have him on.
zh: 你应该把这一段缩短一点然后让他上。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the way I would describe it is, and I tell this to myself as well, which is like, okay, do you drive Notion like it's stolen, which is we're not the founders.
zh: 我想我会描述它的方式是，我也这样告诉自己，这就像，好吧，你像开偷来的车一样开 Notion 吗，也就是我们不是创始人。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're coming in after there was already insane product market fit, but you can still contribute to the company in a way that you feel agency and you're not just like, it's what's your role?
zh: 我们是在已经有了疯狂的产品市场契合度之后进来的，但你仍然可以以一种你感觉到 agency 的方式为公司做出贡献，而你不仅仅像，那你的角色是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so Brian obviously already blurs engineering and design, but he also is probably our number one recruiter in terms of, "Hey, this is what the org needs.
zh: 所以 Brian 显然已经模糊了工程和设计，但他也是我们要的第一名招聘人员，关于，“嘿，这就是组织需要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to go out and talk to people and find someone."
zh: 我要出去和人谈谈，找到一个人。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that is a thing that just demonstrates it's out of the day-to-day and it demonstrates the, "No, I want to just affect change.
zh: 我认为那是一件事，只是表明它超出了日常琐事，并且表明了，“不，我只是想影响改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't care how it happens."
zh: 我不在乎它是如何发生的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Eric Lou is another one, the fact that he went from writing a lot of strategy docs to, he asked me at some point, he's like, "Hey, look, at some point in the future, if you started a startup, would you hire me?"
zh: Eric Lou 是另一个，事实上他从写很多策略文档转变为，他在某个点问我，他就像，“嘿，听着，在未来的某个时间点，如果你创办一家初创公司，你会雇佣我吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I said, "Well, not in the first 10.
zh: 我说，“嗯，不会在前 10 个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't need a product manager."
zh: 我不需要产品经理。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's like, "Oh, okay.
zh: 他就像，“噢，好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to work on the skills so that you would hire me in the first five."
zh: 我要磨练技能，这样你会在前五分钟内录用我。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And that led to first spending more time in Figma instead of writing long PRDs.
zh: 这导致首先在 Figma 上花更多时间，而不是写冗长的 PRD。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now it's just, why do I have to do the Figma thing?
zh: 现在只是，我为什么要做 Figma 那些事？

speaker: Unknown
en: Can't I just build the prototype and at least show you what I think and do the thinking in there?
zh: 我难道不能直接构建原型，至少向你展示我的想法并在那里进行思考吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so those are just sort of signs of high agency of I'm going to change the role to how I think it should be.
zh: 所以这些只是高智能体...的迹象，我要把这个角色变成我认为它应该有的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something you mentioned earlier, which I love, this idea of just rethinking what is this role of engineering, what should it be if we didn't have this meme already for it?
zh: 你早些时候提到的一点，我很喜欢，就是重新思考工程这个角色是什么，如果我们没有关于它的既有观念，它应该是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I wonder what we lose as these roles start to merge.
zh: 我想知道随着这些角色开始融合，我们会失去什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to have this clear engineer, product manager, designer.
zh: 我们过去有清晰的工程师、产品经理、设计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as people start to ... as you talk about malleable software, we'll come back to this, but like malleable roles almost, there's something we lose like clear career paths and design consistency, things like that.
zh: 随着人们开始……正如你谈到可塑软件，我们会回到这一点，但就像可塑的角色一样，我们会失去一些东西，比如清晰的职业路径和设计一致性，诸如此类。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think if we're not careful, we will lose specialists.
zh: 我认为如果我们不小心，我们会失去专家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the way I would describe this is, I sometimes like to think about software in terms of physical metaphors, right?
zh: 所以我描述它的方式是，我有时喜欢用物理隐喻来思考软件，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: And physical metaphors somehow make it so much clearer what a prototype is versus what an engineered thing is.
zh: 物理隐喻不知何故让原型是什么和工程制品是什么变得清晰得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you and I were to build a hardware startup, well, we would make the first enclosures and prototypes with 3D printing and you would see all the layer lines, it would be very, very obvious to you that this is not a thing that you should just give to people to pay for.
zh: 如果你和我建立一个硬件初创公司，好吧，我们会先用 3D 打印制作第一批外壳和原型，你会看到所有的层线，非常非常明显地知道这不是一个应该直接给人们付钱购买的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's a long, windy road all the way to the end where at some point, if you're very lucky, you get to manufacture that product for, I don't know, 100 million people.
zh: 然后有一条漫长、曲折的路一直走到最后，在某个时候，如果你很幸运，你可以为，我不知道，一亿人制造那个产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then the engineering is actually the, how do I optimize the factory so that we have enough yield and so that we have enough precision?
zh: 所以工程实际上是，我如何优化工厂，以便我们有足够的良率，以便我们有足够的精度？

speaker: Unknown
en: And that to me, I think, is very absent right now from most of the discourse in software, which is it's all about how many tokens can we spend and how many features can we ship?
zh: 对我来说，我认为这在目前大部分软件论述中是非常缺席的，这全是关于我们能花多少 token 以及我们能发布多少功能？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm like, okay, but where's the engineering part?
zh: 我会说，好吧，但是工程部分在哪里？

speaker: Unknown
en: And the engineering part is the, you make sure that this thing works for a hundred million people, for a billion people.
zh: 工程部分是，你确保这个东西为一亿人、十亿人工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And on the design side, I think there is the, yes, anyone can now very quickly take a design system off the shelf, build a very usable user interface, get to the core of what's really important, but where's the delight in craft?
zh: 在设计方面，我认为是的，现在任何人都可以很快地拿一个现成的设计系统，构建一个非常可用的用户界面，找到真正重要的核心，但是工艺的乐趣在哪里？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think we have to make sure that we, in this sort of merging of roles, don't lose the specialists on the edges.
zh: 所以我认为我们必须确保在这种角色融合中，不要失去边缘的专家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And yeah, I would say that's something would potentially be sad if we lost it.
zh: 是的，我想说如果我们失去了它，可能会是一件令人悲伤的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to come back to this agency piece because I feel like people hear this word a lot on this podcast.
zh: 我想回到这个智能体部分，因为我觉得人们在这个播客上经常听到这个词。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, agency.
zh: 是的，智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: For someone that wants to build this within themselves or even just understand, do I have agency?
zh: 对于那些想要在内心建立这个，或者只是想理解的人，我有智能体吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think I do.
zh: 我想我有。

speaker: Unknown
en: I imagine everyone listening is like, yes, I am huge.
zh: 我想每一个听众都在说，是的，我很大。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have huge agency.
zh: 我有巨大的智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm such an agent and I'll do what needs to be done.
zh: 我就是这样一个人，我会做需要做的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have a piece of advice for someone that wants to develop this within themselves?
zh: 对于那些想要在自己内心培养这一点的人，你有什么建议吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Partially the reason why I'm in software is the thing that I care most about is the Steve Jobs quote, "One day you wake up and you realize the world is made up by people no smarter than you."
zh: 我从事软件的部分原因是我最关心的是史蒂夫·乔布斯的那句话，"有一天你醒来，你意识到这个世界是由不比你聪明的人组成的。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And there are basically people who realize this by themselves or they have an amazing teacher early on in their life that encourages this.
zh: 基本上有人自己意识到了这一点，或者他们在生命早期有一位了不起的老师鼓励了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the biggest through line I've found is making.
zh: 我发现最大的主线是制作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think if you tinker and if you make things, then you are now on this treadmill of just creating and then you're like, "Oh, it's actually not that hard to learn how to make that chair in my office or let me tweak it a little bit."
zh: 我认为如果你修补和制作东西，那么你现在就在这个创造跑步机上，然后你会说，"哦，学会制作我办公室里的那把椅子实际上并不难，或者让我稍微调整一下。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Or maybe, I don't know, it's like a home cooked meal is a form of tinkering ironically.
zh: 或者也许，我不知道，就像家常便饭是一种讽刺的修补形式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the more you can do that in life, I think actually making things is the innately human toolmaking, creating art and so on.
zh: 我认为你在生活中能做的越多，我认为实际上制作东西是天生的人类制造工具、创作艺术等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: So just do that versus I think when a lot of people hear agency, they think of themselves as they're in this big machine and they're like, "Oh, okay, I'm going to circumvent my terrible boss or manager or whatever so that I get X, Y, and Z."
zh: 所以只要这样做，而不是我想当很多人听到智能体时，他们认为自己处于这台大机器中，他们会说，"哦，好吧，我要绕过我糟糕的老板或经理什么的，这样我就能得到 X、Y 和 Z。"

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, no, no, just start by making things.
zh: 不，不，只需从制作东西开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: And usually, when you get better at making things at some point, people pay attention and it just really awakens you to the idea that you can just change things.
zh: 通常，当你变得擅长制作东西，在某个时候，人们会注意，它真的唤醒了你，你可以改变事情的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this.
zh: 我喜欢这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this meme on Twitter, you could just do things.
zh: Twitter 上有一个梗，你就可以直接做事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this version of it.
zh: 我喜欢这个版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: You could just change things, which is a good segue to something you've been a big, I don't know, advocate of and proponent of this idea of malleable software, something you mentioned earlier.
zh: 你可以直接改变事情，这是一个很好的过渡，你一直是一个，我不知道，这个可塑软件想法的倡导者和支持者，你之前提到过。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like something that wasn't actually possible and now is like, okay, I could see exactly what you're talking about now.
zh: 感觉像是以前不可能的事情，现在是，好吧，我现在能完全明白你在说什么了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've been honest from before the AI revolution.
zh: 你在 AI 革命之前就很诚实。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about just this idea, malleable software, why you think it's so important, what you think people need to be thinking about here.
zh: 谈谈这个想法，可塑软件，为什么你认为它如此重要，你认为人们在这里需要思考什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Malleable software is the idea that software works closer to the interest of the people that use it than the interest of the corporation that makes it, maybe that's how I'd frame it.
zh: 可塑软件的想法是，软件更符合使用它的人的利益，而不是制造它的公司的利益，也许我是这样表述的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in particular, I don't want to use software that is specifically just designed by the ivory tower in Cupertino.
zh: 特别是，我不想使用专门由库比蒂诺象牙塔设计的软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I say this as a huge Apple fanboy, but imagine you lived in an environment where you do not get to rearrange your living room and the kitchen has to be exactly set up the way that someone else decided.
zh: 我作为一个巨大的苹果粉丝说这句话，但想象你住在一个你无法重新布置客厅的环境，厨房必须完全按照别人决定的方式设置。

speaker: Unknown
en: We would not take that, right?
zh: 我们不会接受那个，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: But that is kind of the world that we have in software right now where we have this world of apps and apps are like this very, every layer is glued together of like the user interface, the data ownership and so on.
zh: 但这大致就是我们现在软件界所处的世界，在这个世界里我们有应用世界，应用就像这样，每一层都粘合在一起，比如用户界面、数据所有权等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like this little square on your phone.
zh: 它就像你手机上的那个小方块。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the moment you're like, "Okay, this is a really great app, but I just want to change a little bit."
zh: 当你那一刻就像，“好吧，这真是个很棒的应用，但我只想稍微改变一点点。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That is usually not possible, the behavior.
zh: 这通常是不可能的，那个行为。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have the flip side, which is you could run your own Linux distribution and go that way.
zh: 你有另一面，那就是你可以运行你自己的 Linux 发行版并走那条路。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think then what happens is you realize, "Oh, okay, I like the malleability, but I also have other things to do and I don't always want to start from scratch and figure out why the trackpad doesn't work."
zh: 我想接下来发生的事情是你意识到，“噢，好吧，我喜欢这种可塑性，但我也有其他事情要做，我不想总是从头开始并弄清楚为什么触摸板不工作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so to me, it just comes back down to, do you have ownership over your computing life?
zh: 所以对我来说，这归根结底就是，你对你自己的计算生活拥有所有权吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think increasingly, we don't.
zh: 我认为 increasingly，我们没有。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, you brought this up presumably because I think you may have not thought about malleable software too much before AI, but now you're making your own tools maybe for podcast recording, for prepping for shows or, I don't know, whatever.
zh: 现在，你提出这个想必是因为我认为你可能以前在 AI 之前没有太多思考过可塑软件，但现在你正在制作你自己的工具，也许是播客录制，为了准备节目或者，我不知道，任何东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a myriad examples and people are awakening to this idea of like, "Oh, I can just make tools."
zh: 有无数的例子，人们正在觉醒到这个想法，就像，“噢，我可以只制作工具。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is a form of malleable software, but it has to be built on top of a platform or an operating system that encourages this because otherwise, we're just doing individual ...
zh: 这是可塑软件的一种形式，但它必须建立在一个鼓励这种的平台或操作系统之上，因为否则，我们只是在做个体的……

speaker: Unknown
en: Everybody has their own individual little tool.
zh: 每个人都有他们自己的个体小工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know, I like working with people and I like communal tools.
zh: 我不知道，我喜欢与人合作，我喜欢 communal 工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know, this is a thing that the folks at Ink & Switch are obviously are at the forefront.
zh: 我不知道，这是 Ink & Switch 的人们显然处于前沿的一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I get to work with Geoffrey Litt every single day now that spend a lot of time thinking about how would we make software more malleable so that we feel more ownership over it without going back a long time and not having real-time collaboration and the security aspects and so on.
zh: 我现在每天都能和 Geoffrey Litt 一起工作，他花了很多时间思考我们要如何让软件更具可塑性，以便我们对它感到更多的所有权，而不用回到很长时间并且没有实时协作和安全方面等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really love, and I just want to make sure we highlight this idea you're sharing.
zh: 我真的很爱，我只是想确保我们要强调你正在分享的这个想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's something that I learned also from Brian Chesky at Airbnb, this idea that you can change things, that the things around you are just made by other people that may not actually be smarter than you.
zh: 这也是我从 Airbnb 的 Brian Chesky 那里学到的一件事，这个想法就是你可以改变事物，你周围的事物只是由其他人制造的，他们可能实际上并不比你更聪明。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's just this really empowering thing to always think about that things can change.
zh: 始终考虑事物是可以改变的，这确实是一种非常赋能的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: This isn't the way things have to be forever.
zh: 这不是事情必须永远如此的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: Humans made this thing, like humans made this phone and there are better approaches that other humans ... that you can come up with, other people will come up with.
zh: 是人类制造了这个东西，就像人类制造了这个手机，有更好的方法，其他人……你可以想出，其他人会想出。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I think about as I think about this is there's a video that you pinned to your Twitter profile that we'll link to, which I think is Dieter Rams.
zh: 当我思考这一点时，我想到了一个你固定在你的 Twitter 个人资料上的视频，我们会链接到它，我认为那是 Dieter Rams。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that who the person is?
zh: 那个人是谁？

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's walking around and he's just criticizing all these designed chairs.
zh: 他四处走动，他只是批评所有这些设计好的椅子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about what that video's trying to ... Why you pin that to your profile?
zh: 谈谈那个视频试图……为什么你把它固定在你的个人资料上？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's many reasons.
zh: 有很多原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is, I think maybe the only thing that I have in common with this very accomplished person is that we're both German.
zh: 一个是，我认为也许我和这位非常有成就的人唯一的共同点就是我们都是德国人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so sometimes I joke that I also aspire to disapprovingly just point at things with my walking stick and say, "This isn't good enough.
zh: 所以有时我开玩笑说我也渴望不赞成地用我的手杖指着东西说，“这不够好。

speaker: Unknown
en: This isn't good enough."
zh: 这不够好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason why is because I think if you speak German, this is one of the funniest clips that I've ever ...
zh: 原因是因为我认为如果你会说德语，这是我曾经……最有趣的片段之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just die laughing every single time.
zh: 我每次都笑死了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm actually curious how you think about how it ties to malleable software because the main reason why I use that as a clip of reference is I'm very much in the camp of design should be first useful and then beautiful.
zh: 我实际上很好奇你如何看待它如何联系到可塑软件，因为我使用它作为参考片段的主要原因是，我非常赞成设计应该首先是有用的，然后才是美丽的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think a lot of the pieces there are predominantly things that you put in a museum for display.
zh: 我认为那里的大部分主要是你放在博物馆里展示的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you try to sit on them, you'd be like, "What is this nonsense?"
zh: 如果你试图坐在它们上面，你会像，“这是什么废话？”

speaker: Unknown
en: What I felt there is just like you see all ...
zh: 我在那里感觉到就像你看到所有……

speaker: Unknown
en: I would think it was Frank Gehry and all these famous designers' pieces put up in a museum.
zh: 我会认为是 Frank Gehry 和所有这些著名设计师的作品被放在博物馆里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think to most people, it'd be like, "Wow, this is so incredible and beautiful."
zh: 我认为对大多数人来说，会像，“哇，这太不可思议和美丽了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You see somebody that has a status and a reputation and you assume this is great.
zh: 你看到某人拥有地位和声誉，你假设这是很棒的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I love that he breaks that veil of like, "No, this is so stupid, what is this?
zh: 我喜欢他打破了那个面纱，就像，“不，这太愚蠢了，这是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What does this bunch of cabinets tie together?
zh: 这组柜子系在一起是什么意思？

speaker: Unknown
en: Doesn't make any sense."
zh: 没有任何意义。”

speaker: Unknown
en: For that cabinet, I think he says something like, "It is neither orderly nor properly chaotic."
zh: 对于那个柜子，我认为他说了类似的话，“它既不整齐也不适当的无序。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I understand the connection now.
zh: 我现在理解了那个联系。

speaker: Unknown
en: The timeless way of building and Stewart Brand's How Buildings Learn, I think, idea is also that it's very likely that the best homes for you are not actually built by an architect.
zh: 永恒的建造方式和 Stewart Brand 的 How Buildings Learn，我认为，这个想法也是最适合你的房子很可能不是由建筑师建造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They are the thing that over a long time adapt to exactly how you would love to lead your life and they learn over time versus immediately.
zh: 它们是在很长一段时间内适应你想要过你的生活的东西，它们随着时间的推移而学习，而不是立即。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then that is obviously a very costly version of malleability if you have to rip out a wall or whatever.
zh: 所以这显然是一个非常昂贵的可塑性版本，如果你必须拆掉一面墙或什么的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think the main thing that Dieter Rams points out there is it should be a thing that's useful and a good way to figure out how something is useful is if you can change it and tweak it.
zh: 但我认为 Dieter Rams 指出的主要事情是，它应该是一个有用的东西，而弄清楚某样东西如何有用的一个好方法是如果你可以改变和调整它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Makes sense.
zh: 有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: It all connects.
zh: 它都联系在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: It all connects.
zh: 它都联系在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Max Schoening
en: I get it now.
zh: 我现在明白了。

speaker: Unknown
en: There you go.
zh: 就给你了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we'll link to it.
zh: 我们会把它链接起来。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really funny to watch.
zh: 看着真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wish I understood the German.
zh: 我希望我懂德语。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to come back to this idea of malleable software from a perspective of SaaS and the SaaS apocalypse.
zh: 我想从 SaaS 和 SaaS 末日论的角度回到可延展软件这个概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's all this talk about, we will not need SaaS tools any longer.
zh: 有各种讨论说，我们将不再需要 SaaS 工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: We will build all our own tools.
zh: 我们将构建我们自己的所有工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't need Salesforce.
zh: 我们不需要 Salesforce。

speaker: Unknown
en: Imagine some people are like, "We don't need Notion.
zh: 想象一下有些人会说，“我们不需要 Notion。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to build my own Notion."
zh: 我要构建我自己的 Notion。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You have a hot take there.
zh: 你那里有一个激进的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about what you think is going to happen.
zh: 谈谈你认为会发生什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you just think about what SaaS ...
zh: 如果你只考虑什么是 SaaS……

speaker: Unknown
en: The problem is the moment you have an acronym, it means a lot of very specific things.
zh: 问题在于，一旦你有了首字母缩写，它就意味着很多非常具体的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you're going to say, "Hey, is this type of SaaS that we've built in the 2010s just as relevant as it was in the 2010s?"
zh: 如果你要说，“嘿，我们在 2010 年代构建的这种 SaaS 类型是否像在 2010 年代那样相关？”

speaker: Unknown
en: The answer would be, it would be silly to say, "No, nothing's going to change.
zh: 答案是，如果说“不，什么都不会变。

speaker: Unknown
en: It'll be the same."
zh: 会保持原样。”那就太愚蠢了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I think you can say a lot of SaaS in the 2010s was a very, very fancy form around a spreadsheet or something more generic.
zh: 因为我认为你可以说 2010 年代很多 SaaS 只不过是围绕电子表格或更通用东西的一个非常非常花哨的形式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the thing it did is it just guided people in the right direction to fill out that form, as in it is less malleable than a spreadsheet and that is the value.
zh: 它所做的事情是它只是引导人们朝着正确的方向填写那个表格，也就是说它比电子表格的可延展性差，这就是价值所在。

speaker: Unknown
en: The as a service part is I think the thing that actually matters, which is I don't think most people actually want to maintain the full stack of software.
zh: “即服务”这部分我认为才是真正重要的，也就是我认为大多数人实际上并不想要维护全栈软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so whenever I see someone and I am someone here say, "Oh, I just rebuilt this piece of software."
zh: 所以每当我看到有人——我在这里也是其中之一——说，“哦，我刚刚重建了这个软件。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I've tried rebuilding Notion in a weekend for myself just to push at the edges of frustrating things.
zh: 我曾试过在一个周末内为我个人重建 Notion，只是为了突破那些令人沮丧的事情的边缘。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think people want that.
zh: 我不认为人们想要那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think, for the most part, it's nice if you can just ... People don't want to go hunting either.
zh: 我认为，在很大程度上，如果你能……人们也不想去打猎。

speaker: Unknown
en: They just want to go to Costco and have the steak in a styrofoam packaging and pretend that it wasn't hunting or an animal in the first place.
zh: 他们只想去 Costco，买一块装在泡沫塑料包装里的牛排，并假装这本来就不是打猎来的，也不是动物。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think with software, Bret Taylor says this too, software is like a garden, you need to tend to it.
zh: 我认为对于软件，Bret Taylor 也这么说，软件就像一个花园，你需要照料它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the thing you pay for as a service is the maintenance and a bunch of specialists thinking really hard about a problem.
zh: 你作为服务支付的费用是维护费用，以及一群专家在非常努力地思考一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I don't think that's going away.
zh: 所以我不认为那会消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I would probably say is that tools will become more general.
zh: 我可能会说的是，工具将变得更加通用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I'm obviously biased.
zh: 我是说我显然有偏见。

speaker: Unknown
en: I work at Notion.
zh: 我在 Notion 工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I like Notion and I consider Notion to be fairly malleable, not enough.
zh: 我喜欢 Notion，我认为 Notion 相当可延展，但还不够。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it should become more malleable.
zh: 我认为它应该变得更加可延展。

speaker: Unknown
en: We internally joked, Joanna Stern, a journalist, recently tweeted something along the lines of, "Oh, thanks to Notion AI, I finally understand and use Notion."
zh: 我们内部开玩笑说，记者 Joanna Stern 最近发了一条类似这样的推文，“哦，多亏了 Notion AI，我终于理解并使用 Notion 了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know what that says about Notion.
zh: 我不知道这对 Notion 说明了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And to me, this is a great example of Notion wasn't SaaS in the traditional way.
zh: 对我来说，这是 Notion 不是传统意义上的 SaaS 的一个很好的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's kind of hard to get started, but because of AI now, people can ... they have a tutor essentially and can build more things.
zh: 开始使用有点难，但因为现在有了 AI，人们可以……他们本质上有一个导师，可以构建更多的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I suspect that software will go more back into the '90s of general tools, WordProcessor, spreadsheet, FileMaker Pro, that kind of thing, but those will still be as a service.
zh: 所以我推测软件将更多地回到 90 年代的通用工具，文字处理器、电子表格、FileMaker Pro 之类的东西，但那些仍然会是“即服务”。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you'll still have specialized tools around security and so on of just people who go the extra mile to really solve a user problem.
zh: 然后你仍然会有围绕安全等方面的专用工具，只是那些人付出了额外的努力真正解决用户问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think to some degree the SaaS apocalypse is greatly exaggerated.
zh: 所以我认为在某种程度上 SaaS 末日论被大大夸大了。

speaker: Unknown
en: At the same time, are things going to stay the same?
zh: 同时，事情会保持不变吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Of course not.
zh: 当然不会。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why would they?
zh: 为什么要那样呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: I completely agree.
zh: 我完全同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think people think about just the, okay, I'll create something that's pretty cool and close and then they don't think about exactly as you described.
zh: 我认为人们只考虑，好吧，我会创建一些很酷且接近完成的东西，然后他们就没有像你所描述的那样去思考。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have to maintain this thing forever and I have to keep adding features, taking people's feedback.
zh: 我必须永远维护这个东西，我必须不断添加功能，听取人们的反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the funniest things that I see again and again, I just had the head of product for Claude Code on the podcast, Cat Wu, and she talks about how Slack is basically the OS for Anthropic.
zh: 我一次又一次看到的最有趣的事情之一，我刚刚请 Claude Code 的产品负责人 Cat Wu 上播客，她谈到 Slack 基本上就是 Anthropic 的操作系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything runs through Slack.
zh: 一切都通过 Slack 运行。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you think of all companies that would just like, "We'll just build our own.
zh: 而你会想到所有那些公司都会只是说，“我们就自己构建吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are we doing with Slack?"
zh: 我们在拿 Slack 做什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: No, they're using Slack like crazy.
zh: 不，他们在疯狂地使用 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's just one example of nobody wants to rebuild a tool like Slack and Workday, I think is another example.
zh: 而我认为这只是一个例子，没人想重建像 Slack 这样的工具，Workday 是另一个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's maybe even more unique in the US, but one of the great things about the US is actually specialization.
zh: 我认为这在美国可能甚至更为独特，但美国的好处之一实际上是专业化。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's that I get to spend dollars on something like Notion because it's not that expensive compared to me building it and then why would I waste my time?
zh: 那就是我可以把钱花在像 Notion 这样的东西上，因为相比于我自己构建它，它并不贵，那我为什么要浪费我的时间呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to do other things with my life.
zh: 我想用我的生命做其他事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I don't know, that's not going to go away.
zh: 所以我不知道，这不会消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I agree.
zh: 是的，我同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, people at Anthropic, their time is better spent on building AGI than trying to build better Slack.
zh: 不，Anthropic 的人，他们的时间最好花在构建 AGI 上，而不是试图构建更好的 Slack。

speaker: Unknown
en: I also love the Slack example because, I mean, there's this graphic of what it takes to deliver a notification in Slack, the decision flow chart.
zh: 我也喜欢这个 Slack 的例子，因为，我是说，有一张关于在 Slack 中发送通知需要什么的图表，那个决策流程图。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is just something that you only get to when you have real users, real scale and decades of just, yep, we understand the customer.
zh: 那是只有当你拥有真正的用户、真正的规模以及几十年的，是的，我们理解客户时，才能做到的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to come back to how product building is changing and how it's different.
zh: 我想回到产品构建是如何改变的和它是如何不同的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know you've done a lot of different jobs, but your job, I don't know, a couple years ago, what's most changed?
zh: 我知道你做过很多不同的工作，但你的工作，我不知道，几年前，什么改变得最多？

speaker: Unknown
en: What part is most not something you don't do anymore or you do a lot more of now with AI emerging as a big part of your process?
zh: 哪一部分是你不再做的，或者你现在因为 AI 成为你流程中很大一部分而做得更多的？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the first 10% of every project are now free.
zh: 我认为每个项目的前 10% 现在是免费的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's how I would describe it.
zh: 我会这样描述它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there is no point for most things to, for example, write a ...
zh: 所以对于大多数事情来说，没有意义去，例如，写一个……

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: The thing has changed.
zh: 这件事已经改变了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've never really been great at this, but there's no point in writing a PRD if you can just do the janky version and do the, "Here's the demo of what I think we should build."
zh: 我真的从来没有在这方面很擅长，但是写 PRD 没有任何意义，如果你可以直接做一个简陋的版本并做一个，“这是我认为我们应该构建的演示。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first 10%, that's so interesting.
zh: 所以前 10%，这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's such an interesting way to frame it.
zh: 这是一个非常有趣的框架。

speaker: Unknown
en: Idea, there's just the thinking through of it, you can go a lot further really quickly.
zh: 想法，只是对它的思考，你可以很快地走得更远。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you look at a lot of the ...
zh: 如果你看看很多……

speaker: Unknown
en: It takes almost no effort to now build the first version of a startup or the first version 0.8.
zh: 现在几乎不需要费力就能构建初创公司的第一个版本或第一个 0.8 版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I think the last, or maybe even if you're generous and say the first 90% are now done, the last 10% are still actually 90%.
zh: 然后我认为最后，或者也许即使你慷慨地说前 90% 已经完成了，最后 10% 实际上仍然是 90%。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's always the hardest.
zh: 那总是最难的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it's cheaper to just explore a lot of paths.
zh: 所以我认为探索更多路径变得更便宜了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can now afford to say, "I'm going to send off 10 agents to explore 10 different things and then see if I was right."
zh: 你现在可以负担得起说，“我要派 10 个智能体去探索 10 个不同的东西，然后看看我是否是对的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to say this at GitHub in our product reviews a lot, which is demos not memos.
zh: 我们过去在 GitHub 的产品评审中经常说这一点，那就是演示而不是备忘录。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we would say, give me something to react to, which is, okay, if you're going to write a PRD, just write the change log or the blog post that a user would read.
zh: 然后我们会说，给我一些可以做出反应的东西，也就是，好吧，如果你要写 PRD，就写用户会看到的变更日志或博客文章。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, it's much easier to give people something to react to.
zh: 现在，给人们一些可以做出反应的东西要容易得多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: As in, yeah, here's the version of the product.
zh: 也就是，是的，这是产品的版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like, okay, what if we did it this other way?
zh: 就像是，好吧，如果我们用另一种方式做会怎么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh yeah, here's that version.
zh: 噢是的，这是那个版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think that is just amazing.
zh: 所以我认为这太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It sort of builds in iteration into the product much earlier.
zh: 它将迭代更早地内置到产品中。

speaker: Unknown
en: Waterfall is sort of, why bother?
zh: 瀑布流是，何必呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you think is the next leap or shift in how we build?
zh: 你认为下一个飞跃或转变是什么，关于我们如何构建？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are you seeing as like, okay, this is now the new thing that's emerging that is going to change how we operate?
zh: 你看到了什么，比如，好吧，这是现在正在浮现的、将改变我们运营方式的新事物？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm very conflicted on this because on one hand, I do want to ...
zh: 我对此很矛盾，因为一方面，我确实想要……

speaker: Unknown
en: I believe the never bet against plain text.
zh: 我相信永远不要押注反对纯文本。

speaker: Unknown
en: So a famous forum post at some point.
zh: 所以是某个时候的一个著名论坛帖子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Plain text, markdown.
zh: 纯文本，Markdown。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just such a durable thing.
zh: 这只是一件如此持久的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Code is such a durable thing.
zh: 代码是如此持久的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that expressing your thoughts in code is probably a really good thing.
zh: 我认为用代码表达你的想法可能是一件非常好的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: We can talk about why.
zh: 我们可以讨论为什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: But at the same time, I'm like, are we really going to just be chatting back and forth?
zh: 但与此同时,我在想,我们真的要来回聊天吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what is the future of Figma, for example, is a really interesting example to me because on one hand, I do see a drop in usage of Figma in some designers at Notion.
zh: 所以 Figma 的未来是什么,对我来说是一个非常有意思的例子,因为一方面,我确实看到 Notion 的某些设计师对 Figma 的使用下降了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then others are like, "Nope, these AI tools are wonderful."
zh: 然后其他人会说,"不,这些 AI 工具很棒。"

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very hard for me to predict of like, is direct manipulation going away because the agent is doing the direct manipulation?
zh: 对我来说很难预测,直接操作是否正在消失,因为智能体正在做直接操作？

speaker: Unknown
en: The other thing that I'm curious about is there is this automation versus augmentation fork.
zh: 我好奇的另一件事是自动化与增强的岔路口。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I look at the really, really fast models like Spark, and I forget what the Anthropic variant is where-
zh: 如果我看那些非常、非常快的模型,比如 Spark,我忘了 Anthropic 的变体是哪个——

speaker: Unknown
en: Haiku.
zh: Haiku。

speaker: Unknown
en: No.
zh: 不。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sorry.
zh: 抱歉。
